cards.definition: a Greek doctor whose ideas dominated medicine for over 1000 years — taught that the body had four humours and that doctors should use observation
the medieval theory that the body contained four fluids — blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile — illness occurred when these were imbalanced
the belief that disease was caused by bad air — widely accepted in the medieval and early modern period
a common medical treatment to rebalance the humours — doctors removed blood by cutting veins or using leeches
the dominant institution in medieval society — controlled medical training, supported Galen's ideas, and provided hospital care
a medieval medical practitioner who prepared and sold herbal remedies — similar to a pharmacist
a medieval practitioner who performed minor surgery and pulled teeth — lower status than physicians
a devastating plague killing around 1/3 of England's population — miasma theory could not explain it and treatments failed
a university-trained doctor who studied classical texts — the highest-status medieval medical practitioner
belief in supernatural explanations for illness — prayers, charms, and pilgrimages were used as cures

questions.question: What were the four humours?
options: A) Fire, water, earth, air
B) Blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile
C) Heart, brain, lungs, liver
D) Hot, cold, wet, dry
questions.explanation: The four humours theory: blood (hot/wet), phlegm (cold/wet), yellow bile (hot/dry), black bile (cold/dry). Illness = imbalance.

questions.question: What was miasma theory?
options: A) The belief that illness spread through touch
B) The belief that disease was caused by bad air
C) The idea that the stars caused illness
D) The theory that insects spread disease
questions.explanation: Miasma theory held that disease came from foul-smelling air — it discouraged investigation of the real causes of disease.

questions.question: Why did the Church support Galen's ideas?
options: A) Galen was a Christian
B) Galen's ideas were scientifically proven
C) Galen's ideas fitted with Church teachings about God creating the human body
D) The Church banned all other medical theories
questions.explanation: Galen believed the body was perfectly designed — this fitted with Christian beliefs about God's creation, so the Church endorsed his ideas.

questions.question: What was bloodletting used for?
options: A) To fight infection
B) To remove toxins
C) To rebalance the humours by removing excess blood
D) To reduce fever by cooling the body
questions.explanation: Bloodletting was based on the humour theory — if blood was in excess, removing it would restore balance. It was often harmful.

questions.question: What was the role of the apothecary?
options: A) Performed surgery
B) Trained physicians at universities
C) Prepared and sold herbal remedies
D) Provided hospital nursing care
questions.explanation: Apothecaries were herbal remedy makers and sellers — they were lower status than physicians but provided accessible treatments.

questions.question: How did the Black Death challenge medieval medicine?
options: A) It proved Galen's theories were correct
B) It showed the Church had no role in medicine
C) Treatments based on humours and miasma theory completely failed
D) It led to immediate advances in understanding disease
questions.explanation: The Black Death (1348) killed ~1/3 of England — miasma-based treatments, prayers, and bloodletting all failed. Medicine could not explain or stop it.

questions.question: What did physicians mainly study?
options: A) Human dissection
B) Ancient texts — especially Galen and Hippocrates
C) Practical surgery
D) Herbal remedies
questions.explanation: Medieval physicians trained at university by reading ancient Greek and Islamic texts — observation and practical dissection were rare.

questions.question: Why was the Church important to medieval medicine?
options: A) The Church funded medical research
B) The Church ran universities, hospitals, and supported Galen — controlling what was taught
C) The Church encouraged scientific experiment
D) The Church trained all barber-surgeons
questions.explanation: The Church controlled universities and hospitals, endorsed Galen, and taught that illness could be God's punishment — suppressing new ideas.

questions.question: What was a barber surgeon?
options: A) A university-trained physician
B) A practitioner who performed minor surgery, bloodletting, and pulled teeth
C) A nurse in a monastery hospital
D) An Islamic medical scholar
questions.explanation: Barber surgeons were low-status practitioners — they performed practical procedures like bloodletting, tooth extraction, and wound treatment.

questions.question: What supernatural treatments were used in medieval medicine?
options: A) Dissection and anatomy
B) Chemical remedies
C) Prayer, pilgrimage, charms, and amulets
D) Vaccination and inoculation
questions.explanation: Medieval medicine blended natural and supernatural explanations — prayer, pilgrimages to saints, and wearing amulets were as common as herbal remedies.

questions.question: The four humours theory was developed by Galen.
questions.explanation: The four humours theory was developed by Hippocrates and further developed by Galen.

questions.question: Miasma theory blamed bad air for causing disease.
questions.explanation: Miasma was the dominant explanation for disease from ancient times through to the mid-19th century.

questions.question: The Church encouraged dissection and new medical ideas.
questions.explanation: The Church discouraged challenging Galen and initially opposed human dissection — it controlled medical thought.

questions.question: Bloodletting was designed to restore the balance of the humours.
questions.explanation: Removing blood treated supposed excess of the blood humour — it was based on humour theory and was often harmful.

questions.question: Barber surgeons had higher status than physicians.
questions.explanation: Physicians (university-trained) had the highest status. Barber surgeons were low-status practical practitioners.

questions.question: The Black Death killed approximately one third of England's population.
questions.explanation: The Black Death (1348) was catastrophic — it killed around a third of England's population and existing treatments were useless.

questions.question: Apothecaries prepared and sold herbal remedies.
questions.explanation: Apothecaries were the medieval equivalent of pharmacists — they made and sold plant-based medicines.

questions.question: Medieval medicine relied entirely on observation and experiment.
questions.explanation: Medieval medicine relied on ancient texts and authority — observation and experiment were not valued in the way they are today.

questions.question: Galen's ideas were used in medicine for over 1000 years.
questions.explanation: Galen's theories (c.130–210 AD) dominated Western medicine until the Renaissance — supported by the Church's authority.

questions.question: Superstition played no role in medieval medicine.
questions.explanation: Superstition was central — illness could be God's punishment, treated by prayer, pilgrimage, and charms alongside physical remedies.

questions.question: The ________ humours were blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile.
questions.explanation: Imbalance of the humours was believed to cause illness — restoring balance was the aim of treatment.

questions.question: ________ theory blamed disease on foul-smelling bad air.
questions.explanation: Miasma discouraged investigation of real causes — it persisted until germ theory in the 1860s.

questions.question: The ________ supported Galen's ideas because they fitted with Christian belief.
questions.explanation: The Church's endorsement of Galen suppressed new medical ideas for centuries.

questions.question: ________ was a treatment to rebalance the humours by removing blood.
questions.explanation: Doctors used knives or leeches — it was often harmful and rarely effective.

questions.question: A ________ was a university-trained doctor who studied ancient texts.
questions.explanation: Physicians were the highest-status medical practitioners in medieval society.

questions.question: The ________ Death of 1348 killed around a third of England's population.
questions.explanation: The Black Death showed the complete failure of medieval medicine to understand or treat epidemic disease.

questions.question: A ________ performed minor surgery and tooth extractions.
questions.explanation: Barber surgeons were low-status practical practitioners.

questions.question: An ________ prepared and sold herbal remedies — similar to a pharmacist.
questions.explanation: Apothecaries were accessible to ordinary people who could not afford physicians.

questions.question: Medieval patients used prayer, ________, and charms alongside physical treatments.
questions.explanation: Supernatural treatments were taken just as seriously as physical ones.

questions.question: ________ ideas dominated Western medicine for over 1000 years.
questions.explanation: Galen wrote extensively on anatomy, physiology, and treatment — his errors were not challenged for centuries.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile — imbalance causes illness
disease caused by bad air
removing blood to rebalance humours
controlled medical training — supported Galen
killed ~1/3 of England — humour-based treatments failed

cards.definition: a 16th-century Flemish anatomist who proved Galen wrong through human dissection — published 'The Fabric of the Human Body' (1543)
proved that blood circulates around the body — published 'On the Motion of the Heart' (1628) — challenged Galen's ideas on blood
a French surgeon who improved wound treatment — used turpentine ointment instead of boiling oil and developed artificial limbs
Gutenberg's invention (c.1440) — allowed new medical ideas to spread quickly across Europe
developed vaccination against smallpox in 1796 — observed that milkmaids who caught cowpox were protected from smallpox
Pasteur's discovery (1861) that microorganisms cause disease — overturned miasma theory and transformed medicine
built on Pasteur's germ theory — identified specific bacteria causing diseases including TB (1882) and cholera (1883)
traced a cholera outbreak to a water pump in Soho (1854) — evidence-based epidemiology before germ theory was proven
chemicals that remove the feeling of pain during surgery — ether (1842) and chloroform (1847) enabled longer, more complex operations
chemicals that kill bacteria and prevent infection — Joseph Lister developed carbolic spray (1867) based on germ theory

questions.question: What did Vesalius prove?
options: A) That blood circulates
B) That Galen was wrong about human anatomy through dissection
C) That germs cause disease
D) That the printing press spread disease
questions.explanation: Vesalius dissected human bodies and found errors in Galen — e.g. the human jaw is one bone, not two. His work challenged 1400 years of accepted anatomy.

questions.question: What did William Harvey prove?
options: A) The four humours were correct
B) That vaccination worked
C) That blood circulates continuously around the body
D) That germs cause infection
questions.explanation: Harvey proved blood circulates — pumped by the heart, moving in one direction. This contradicted Galen's idea that blood was consumed by the body.

questions.question: How did the printing press help medicine?
options: A) It prevented the spread of disease
B) It allowed new medical ideas to spread rapidly across Europe
C) It replaced handwritten medical texts
D) It made medicine affordable for the poor
questions.explanation: Before printing, books were hand-copied — few existed. The printing press allowed Vesalius and Harvey's ideas to spread widely and quickly.

questions.question: What was Edward Jenner's contribution?
options: A) He discovered germ theory
B) He developed anaesthetics
C) He developed vaccination against smallpox using cowpox
D) He proved blood circulates
questions.explanation: Jenner observed that milkmaids with cowpox didn't get smallpox — he tested vaccination in 1796 and it worked, though he didn't know why (germ theory came later).

questions.question: What did Louis Pasteur discover?
options: A) That blood circulates
B) That germs (microorganisms) cause disease
C) That vaccination prevents smallpox
D) That antiseptics prevent infection
questions.explanation: Pasteur's germ theory (1861) showed that microorganisms cause fermentation and disease — overturning miasma theory and transforming medicine.

questions.question: What was the significance of anaesthetics?
options: A) They prevented wound infection
B) They proved germ theory
C) They removed pain, allowing longer and more complex operations
D) They were used to treat cholera
questions.explanation: Before anaesthetics, surgery was extremely painful and fast — patients were held down. Ether and chloroform allowed painless, longer operations.

questions.question: What did Joseph Lister do?
options: A) Developed vaccination
B) Identified specific bacteria causing diseases
C) Applied germ theory to surgery using carbolic acid antiseptic spray
D) Proved blood circulates
questions.explanation: Lister applied Pasteur's germ theory to surgery — his carbolic acid spray killed bacteria during operations, dramatically reducing death from infection.

questions.question: How did John Snow contribute to understanding disease?
options: A) He developed germ theory
B) He traced a cholera outbreak to a contaminated water pump — evidence-based epidemiology
C) He proved that miasma caused cholera
D) He developed antiseptic surgery
questions.explanation: Snow mapped cholera cases in Soho and found they clustered around one water pump — removing the handle stopped the outbreak. This was evidence-based medicine before germ theory.

questions.question: What did Robert Koch contribute?
options: A) He developed the smallpox vaccine
B) He identified specific bacteria causing TB and cholera
C) He invented antiseptic surgery
D) He proved blood circulates
questions.explanation: Koch developed techniques to isolate and identify specific bacteria — proving that individual bacteria caused specific diseases. This enabled targeted treatments.

questions.question: What was the problem with early anaesthetics?
options: A) They didn't work
B) They were too expensive
C) They removed pain but not infection — surgery death rates initially rose
D) They were illegal
questions.explanation: Anaesthetics solved the pain problem but increased blood loss and operating time — without antiseptics to tackle infection, death rates from surgery initially increased.

questions.question: Vesalius proved Galen correct through human dissection.
questions.explanation: Vesalius proved Galen WRONG — he found errors in Galen's anatomy through systematic human dissection.

questions.question: William Harvey proved blood circulates around the body.
questions.explanation: Harvey showed blood is pumped by the heart in one direction around the body — contradicting Galen's theory.

questions.question: The printing press slowed the spread of new medical ideas.
questions.explanation: The printing press accelerated the spread of ideas — Vesalius and Harvey's discoveries spread rapidly across Europe.

questions.question: Jenner understood the scientific basis for why vaccination worked.
questions.explanation: Jenner observed that vaccination worked but did not know why — germ theory and viruses were discovered much later.

questions.question: Germ theory overturned miasma theory.
questions.explanation: Pasteur's germ theory (1861) proved that microorganisms cause disease — ending the dominance of miasma theory.

questions.question: Anaesthetics immediately solved all problems with surgery.
questions.explanation: Anaesthetics removed pain but without antiseptics, infection remained fatal. Surgery death rates initially rose.

questions.question: Lister used carbolic acid to prevent infection during surgery.
questions.explanation: Lister's carbolic spray killed bacteria during operations — applying germ theory to surgical practice.

questions.question: John Snow proved germ theory before Pasteur.
questions.explanation: Snow identified contaminated water as the source of cholera in 1854 — but without knowing about germs. Pasteur published germ theory in 1861.

questions.question: Koch identified specific bacteria causing TB and cholera.
questions.explanation: Koch developed techniques to isolate bacteria — his identification of the TB bacillus (1882) was a landmark.

questions.question: Paré introduced boiling oil as a standard wound treatment.
questions.explanation: Paré REPLACED boiling oil with a gentler turpentine ointment after running out of oil — his patients did better.

questions.question: ________ proved Galen wrong about anatomy through systematic human dissection.
questions.explanation: His 1543 work 'The Fabric of the Human Body' contained accurate anatomical drawings.

questions.question: William ________ proved that blood circulates continuously, pumped by the heart.
questions.explanation: Harvey used dissection and experiment — his 1628 publication challenged Galenic ideas.

questions.question: The ________ press allowed new medical ideas to spread rapidly across Europe.
questions.explanation: Without printing, Vesalius and Harvey's discoveries would have spread much more slowly.

questions.question: Edward ________ developed vaccination against smallpox using cowpox in 1796.
questions.explanation: Jenner's empirical observation — milkmaids with cowpox didn't get smallpox — led to the first vaccine.

questions.question: Pasteur's ________ theory proved that microorganisms cause disease.
questions.explanation: Germ theory (1861) transformed medicine — explaining infection, enabling vaccines, and guiding surgery.

questions.question: ________ developed carbolic acid antiseptic spray to prevent surgical infection.
questions.explanation: Lister applied germ theory to surgery — dramatically reducing post-operative death from infection.

questions.question: Robert ________ identified specific bacteria causing TB and cholera.
questions.explanation: Koch's techniques allowed scientists to link specific bacteria to specific diseases.

questions.question: ________ traced cholera to a contaminated water pump in Soho (1854).
questions.explanation: Snow's epidemiological mapping was evidence-based medicine before germ theory was proven.

questions.question: ________ allowed painless surgery — ether and chloroform were key early examples.
questions.explanation: Before anaesthetics, operations had to be extremely fast — patients were conscious and in agony.

questions.question: ________ are chemicals that kill bacteria — Lister's carbolic acid was a key example.
questions.explanation: Antiseptics solved the infection problem in surgery — transforming survival rates.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: proved Galen wrong through human dissection (1543)
proved blood circulates (1628)
Pasteur — microorganisms cause disease (1861)
vaccination against smallpox (1796)
carbolic antiseptic spray — prevents surgical infection (1867)

cards.definition: Germany's democratic government (1919–1933) — established after WWI defeat; seen by many as weak and illegitimate
the peace treaty ending WWI — Germany was blamed for the war, lost territory, paid reparations, and had its army restricted
the false belief that Germany lost WWI because of betrayal by Jews and socialists — used by Hitler to undermine the Republic
catastrophic inflation in Germany — the Mark became worthless; savings wiped out; caused by printing money to pay reparations
a global economic crisis — caused mass unemployment in Germany, increasing support for extreme parties including the Nazis
the Weimar voting system — gave extremist parties seats in parliament; made coalition governments unstable
Hitler's failed attempt to seize power in Munich — he was arrested, tried, and used the trial to gain national attention
'My Struggle' — Hitler's autobiographical manifesto, written in prison — outlined his ideology including anti-Semitism and lebensraum
the Nazi paramilitary force — used violence and intimidation against political opponents
information used to promote a particular viewpoint — Goebbels masterminded Nazi propaganda through posters, rallies, and radio

questions.question: What was the stab in the back myth?
options: A) A true account of Germany's military defeat
B) The false belief that Jews and socialists betrayed Germany in WWI
C) Hitler's account of the Munich Putsch
D) Germany's border with France
questions.explanation: The stab-in-the-back myth (Dolchstoßlegende) was a false narrative — Germany's military leaders spread it to avoid blame for defeat. Hitler used it to attack the Weimar Republic.

questions.question: What were the main terms of the Treaty of Versailles?
options: A) Germany gained territory and rebuilt its army
B) Germany paid reparations, lost territory, restricted its army, and accepted war guilt
C) Germany was split into two countries
D) Germany was forgiven all war debts
questions.explanation: The Treaty's key terms: war guilt clause (Article 231), £6.6 billion reparations, loss of 13% of territory, army limited to 100,000 men — it was deeply resented in Germany.

questions.question: What caused hyperinflation in 1923?
options: A) The Wall Street Crash
B) Germany printing money to pay reparations, causing the Mark to collapse
C) Food shortages causing price rises
D) Unemployment reducing tax revenues
questions.explanation: Germany printed money to pay reparations after France occupied the Ruhr — the Mark became virtually worthless. By November 1923 it took billions of Marks to buy bread.

questions.question: How did the Great Depression help Hitler?
options: A) It made Germans support the Weimar Republic
B) It caused mass unemployment, increasing support for extreme parties including the Nazis
C) It destroyed the SA
D) It allowed Germany to renegotiate reparations
questions.explanation: By 1932 there were 6 million unemployed in Germany. Desperate voters turned to extreme parties — Nazi support surged from 12 seats (1928) to 230 seats (July 1932).

questions.question: What was the Munich Putsch?
options: A) Hitler's election victory in 1933
B) Hitler's failed attempt to seize power in Munich in 1923
C) The Nazis' violent attack on Jewish businesses
D) The burning of the Reichstag
questions.explanation: Hitler tried to march on Berlin from Munich in 1923, inspired by Mussolini. It failed — he was arrested. But his trial gave him national publicity and he used prison to write Mein Kampf.

questions.question: What was proportional representation?
options: A) A system giving large parties extra seats
B) A voting system giving parties seats based on their share of the vote
C) The method of electing the President
D) A system used to elect local councils only
questions.explanation: Proportional representation meant small extreme parties like the Nazis could win seats. It also made stable majorities difficult — coalition governments collapsed repeatedly.

questions.question: What was the SA?
options: A) The Nazi secret police
B) Hitler's personal bodyguard
C) The Nazi paramilitary force used to intimidate opponents
D) The German regular army
questions.explanation: The SA (Sturmabteilung) were the Nazi stormtroopers — they attacked political opponents, disrupted rival meetings, and spread fear. Essential in the early rise of Nazism.

questions.question: What was the role of propaganda in Nazi success?
options: A) It played no significant role
B) Goebbels used posters, rallies, and radio to spread Nazi ideology and create a cult of Hitler
C) It was only used after Hitler came to power
D) It was primarily aimed at foreign countries
questions.explanation: Goebbels was the master of Nazi propaganda — using every medium available to promote Hitler as Germany's saviour and associate Jews and communists with Germany's problems.

questions.question: What did Hitler write in Mein Kampf?
options: A) Germany's new constitution
B) His autobiography and ideology — anti-Semitism, nationalism, and lebensraum
C) The Weimar Republic's laws
D) Economic plans for ending unemployment
questions.explanation: Mein Kampf (1925) outlined Hitler's worldview: racial hierarchy with Aryans at the top, Jews as the enemy, the need for lebensraum (living space) in the East, and extreme nationalism.

questions.question: What made the Weimar Republic politically unstable?
options: A) Its strong military
B) Too many powerful political parties
C) Proportional representation, economic crises, and lack of public confidence
D) Interference from France
questions.explanation: The Weimar Republic faced: opposition from left and right, economic crises (1923, 1929), a voting system producing unstable coalitions, and the stab-in-the-back myth undermining its legitimacy.

questions.question: The Weimar Republic was Germany's democratic government after WWI.
questions.explanation: The Weimar Republic (1919–1933) replaced the Kaiser's autocratic rule — but it was seen as weak and imposed by the Allies.

questions.question: The stab in the back myth was based on truth.
questions.explanation: The stab-in-the-back myth was false — Germany was militarily defeated. The myth was invented to avoid blame and undermine the democratic government.

questions.question: Hyperinflation in 1923 made the Mark more valuable.
questions.explanation: Hyperinflation made the Mark almost worthless — prices rose billions of times. Savings were wiped out.

questions.question: The Great Depression increased support for extreme political parties.
questions.explanation: Mass unemployment and desperation made voters turn to extreme parties — Nazi support surged after 1929.

questions.question: The Munich Putsch was Hitler's successful seizure of power.
questions.explanation: The Munich Putsch FAILED — Hitler was arrested and imprisoned. But his trial gave him national publicity.

questions.question: Proportional representation helped create political instability.
questions.explanation: PR gave small parties seats and made stable majorities difficult — coalition governments collapsed repeatedly.

questions.question: Goebbels was responsible for Nazi propaganda.
questions.explanation: Joseph Goebbels was Hitler's Minister of Propaganda — he used radio, posters, rallies, and film to spread Nazi ideology.

questions.question: Mein Kampf was written after Hitler came to power.
questions.explanation: Mein Kampf was written while Hitler was in prison after the Munich Putsch (1923–1924).

questions.question: The Treaty of Versailles was popular in Germany.
questions.explanation: The Treaty was deeply resented — Germans called it a Diktat (dictated peace). The war guilt clause and reparations caused widespread anger.

questions.question: The SA was used to intimidate political opponents.
questions.explanation: The SA's violence and street battles against communists and socialists were central to the Nazi rise to power.

questions.question: The ________ Republic was Germany's democratic government from 1919 to 1933.
questions.explanation: The Weimar Republic was named after the city where its constitution was written.

questions.question: The ________ of Versailles blamed Germany for WWI and imposed heavy reparations.
questions.explanation: The Treaty's terms caused widespread resentment — helping extremist parties gain support.

questions.question: The stab in the ________ myth falsely blamed Jews and socialists for Germany's defeat.
questions.explanation: This myth undermined the Weimar Republic and was exploited by Hitler.

questions.question: ________ in 1923 made the German Mark worthless — savings were destroyed.
questions.explanation: Germany printed money to pay reparations — causing catastrophic inflation.

questions.question: The ________ Putsch (1923) was Hitler's failed attempt to seize power.
questions.explanation: The failed coup gave Hitler national publicity and time in prison to write Mein Kampf.

questions.question: ________ ________ was the voting system that gave extreme parties parliamentary seats.
questions.explanation: PR made stable governments difficult — contributing to political instability.

questions.question: Hitler wrote ________ ________ in prison — it outlined his anti-Semitic ideology.
questions.explanation: 'My Struggle' set out Hitler's worldview — racial hierarchy, anti-Semitism, and lebensraum.

questions.question: The ________ was the Nazi paramilitary force that intimidated political opponents.
questions.explanation: SA stormtroopers used violence to disrupt rivals and enforce Nazi dominance on the streets.

questions.question: Goebbels used posters, rallies, and ________ to spread Nazi propaganda.
questions.explanation: The radio was particularly powerful — Hitler's speeches could reach millions of German homes.

questions.question: The Great ________ of 1929 caused mass unemployment and boosted Nazi support.
questions.explanation: By 1932, 6 million Germans were unemployed — desperation drove voters to extreme parties.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: Germany's democratic government 1919–1933 — unstable and resented
blamed Germany — reparations, territory loss, army restrictions
Mark became worthless — savings destroyed
Hitler's failed coup — gained him national attention
mass unemployment — boosted Nazi support

cards.definition: the state of tension and rivalry between the USA and USSR from 1945–1991 — never direct military conflict between the two superpowers
the US economic system — private ownership, free market, democracy
the Soviet economic system — state ownership, planned economy, one-party rule
meeting between Stalin, Roosevelt, and Churchill — agreed post-war Europe, free elections in Eastern Europe, and the United Nations
follow-up conference — Truman replaced Roosevelt; disagreements over Germany and reparations increased tension
Churchill's term (1946) for the divide between Soviet-controlled Eastern Europe and free Western Europe
US policy to contain communism — promised aid to countries threatened by communist takeover
US economic aid to rebuild Western Europe — $13 billion; strengthened capitalism and countered Soviet influence
Stalin blockaded West Berlin — the West responded with the Berlin Airlift; first major Cold War confrontation
the North Atlantic Treaty Organisation — Western military alliance; a direct response to Soviet aggression

questions.question: What was the Cold War?
options: A) A series of direct wars between the USA and USSR
B) A state of tension and rivalry — never direct military conflict between superpowers
C) A nuclear war in the 1960s
D) Economic cooperation between the USA and USSR
questions.explanation: The Cold War was a period of rivalry, proxy wars, and arms race — but the USA and USSR never directly fought each other, fearing nuclear war.

questions.question: What was agreed at the Yalta Conference?
options: A) The dropping of the atomic bomb
B) Post-war Germany divided into zones, free elections in Eastern Europe, and the UN
C) The invasion of Japan
D) Germany to be completely destroyed
questions.explanation: At Yalta (Feb 1945) — Germany divided into zones, Stalin promised free elections in Eastern Europe (not kept), and the UN was agreed.

questions.question: What was the Truman Doctrine?
options: A) A plan to rebuild Western Europe
B) A US commitment to containing communism by supporting threatened nations
C) An agreement to limit nuclear weapons
D) A plan to unite Germany
questions.explanation: The Truman Doctrine (1947) committed the USA to supporting any country threatened by communist takeover — beginning with Greece and Turkey.

questions.question: What was the Marshall Plan?
options: A) A US military alliance against the USSR
B) A Soviet economic plan for Eastern Europe
C) US economic aid of $13 billion to rebuild Western Europe
D) A plan to divide Germany
questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan offered $13 billion to rebuild European economies — strengthening democracy and capitalism, and reducing the appeal of communism.

questions.question: What was the Iron Curtain?
options: A) A literal wall dividing Europe
B) Churchill's term for the divide between Soviet-controlled East and free West
C) A Soviet military defence line
D) A US economic barrier to Soviet goods
questions.explanation: Churchill's 1946 speech coined the term 'Iron Curtain' to describe the boundary between Soviet-dominated Eastern Europe and the democratic West.

questions.question: What caused the Berlin Blockade?
options: A) The USSR wanted to take over West Berlin by force
B) Stalin blockaded West Berlin to try to force the West to abandon it
C) The West blockaded East Berlin
D) Germany requested the blockade
questions.explanation: Stalin blockaded road and rail links to West Berlin (June 1948) to force the West to abandon the city — the West responded with the Berlin Airlift, supplying the city by air.

questions.question: What was NATO?
options: A) A Soviet military alliance
B) A Western military alliance created in response to Soviet aggression
C) A United Nations peacekeeping force
D) An economic organisation for Western Europe
questions.explanation: NATO (1949) was a Western defensive alliance — an attack on one member was an attack on all. It was a direct response to Soviet expansion.

questions.question: Why did the USA implement the Marshall Plan?
options: A) To repay war debts
B) To rebuild European economies and reduce the appeal of communism
C) To create a military alliance
D) To punish Germany for WWII
questions.explanation: A prosperous, stable Western Europe was less likely to turn communist — the Marshall Plan combined genuine humanitarian aid with US strategic interests.

questions.question: What was the significance of the Potsdam Conference?
options: A) It established the Iron Curtain
B) It created NATO
C) Disagreements over Germany between Truman and Stalin increased Cold War tension
D) It agreed the Marshall Plan
questions.explanation: Potsdam (July 1945) was more tense than Yalta — Truman was less cooperative than Roosevelt, the USA had the atomic bomb, and disagreements over Germany widened the rift.

questions.question: What were the ideological differences between the USA and USSR?
options: A) Both were capitalist but with different political systems
B) The USA was communist; the USSR was capitalist
C) USA = capitalism/democracy; USSR = communism/one-party state
D) They shared the same economic system
questions.explanation: The USA and USSR had fundamentally opposed ideologies — capitalism vs communism, democracy vs one-party state. This made cooperation after WWII very difficult.

questions.question: The Cold War involved direct military conflict between the USA and USSR.
questions.explanation: The Cold War never became a 'hot war' between the superpowers — both sides feared nuclear war. Conflict was indirect through proxy wars.

questions.question: The Yalta Conference agreed on free elections in Eastern Europe.
questions.explanation: Stalin promised free elections at Yalta — he did not keep this promise, installing communist governments across Eastern Europe.

questions.question: The Marshall Plan was a Soviet initiative to rebuild Eastern Europe.
questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan was a US plan to rebuild WESTERN Europe with $13 billion — it was rejected by Stalin and countries under Soviet influence.

questions.question: The Iron Curtain was a literal physical barrier.
questions.explanation: The Iron Curtain was Churchill's metaphor for the ideological divide — later partly made physical by the Berlin Wall (1961).

questions.question: The Truman Doctrine committed the USA to containing communism.
questions.explanation: The Truman Doctrine (1947) pledged US support to any country threatened by communist takeover.

questions.question: Stalin's Berlin Blockade was an attempt to force the West out of Berlin.
questions.explanation: Stalin blockaded road and rail access to West Berlin hoping the West would abandon the city rather than face conflict.

questions.question: NATO was created as a defensive military alliance against Soviet aggression.
questions.explanation: NATO (1949) committed Western nations to collective defence — if one was attacked, all would respond.

questions.question: The Potsdam Conference was more cooperative than Yalta.
questions.explanation: Potsdam was MORE tense — Truman was less conciliatory, the USA had the atomic bomb, and disagreements over Germany worsened.

questions.question: Communism and capitalism are compatible economic systems.
questions.explanation: Communism (state ownership, planned economy) and capitalism (private ownership, free market) are fundamentally opposed ideologies.

questions.question: The Berlin Airlift successfully supplied West Berlin during the blockade.
questions.explanation: The West airlifted supplies to West Berlin for almost a year — Stalin lifted the blockade in May 1949 having failed to drive the West out.

questions.question: The ________ War was a period of tension between the USA and USSR — never direct military conflict.
questions.explanation: The Cold War lasted from 1945 to 1991 — it shaped global politics for nearly 50 years.

questions.question: At ________, Stalin, Roosevelt, and Churchill agreed Germany would be divided into zones.
questions.explanation: Yalta (February 1945) also agreed free elections in Eastern Europe and the creation of the UN.

questions.question: The ________ Doctrine committed the USA to supporting countries threatened by communism.
questions.explanation: The Truman Doctrine (1947) began with support for Greece and Turkey.

questions.question: The ________ Plan gave $13 billion of US aid to rebuild Western Europe.
questions.explanation: The Marshall Plan strengthened capitalism and reduced communist appeal in Western Europe.

questions.question: The '________ Curtain' was Churchill's term for the divide between East and West Europe.
questions.explanation: Churchill's 1946 speech used the Iron Curtain metaphor to describe Soviet control of Eastern Europe.

questions.question: Stalin's ________ Blockade tried to force the West out of West Berlin.
questions.explanation: The Berlin Airlift defeated the blockade — Stalin backed down in May 1949.

questions.question: ________ was a Western defensive military alliance created in 1949.
questions.explanation: NATO committed members to collective defence — an attack on one was an attack on all.

questions.question: The ________ Conference (1945) was more tense than Yalta — increasing Cold War tension.
questions.explanation: Truman's tougher stance and the atomic bomb increased Soviet suspicion at Potsdam.

questions.question: The USA favoured ________ — private ownership, free market, and democracy.
questions.explanation: Capitalist democracy was seen by the USA as the best system — spreading it globally was a key goal.

questions.question: The USSR favoured ________ — state ownership, planned economy, and one-party rule.
questions.explanation: Soviet communism was fundamentally opposed to capitalism — this ideological clash drove the Cold War.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: USA vs USSR rivalry 1945–1991 — no direct military conflict
agreed post-war Europe — Stalin promised free elections
USA to contain communism globally
$13 billion US aid to rebuild Western Europe
Stalin tried to force West out — defeated by airlift

cards.definition: William of Normandy defeated Harold II — changing England's language, culture, and ruling class
the Norman system of land ownership — King at top, then barons, knights, peasants — based on loyalty and military service
William I's survey of England — recorded land ownership, resources, and value — used for taxation
a Norman military and administrative base — motte and bailey initially, then stone keeps — symbols of Norman power
Duke of Normandy who won the Battle of Hastings — became William I of England, imposing Norman rule
the last Anglo-Saxon king of England — killed at Hastings, possibly by an arrow to the eye
the Great Charter — King John forced to limit his own power by the barons — an early step towards democracy
Edward of Woodstock — a famous English military leader in the Hundred Years' War; son of Edward III
the lowest class under the feudal system — bound to the land (serfs); owed labour to their lord
a powerful institution controlling education, legal courts, and spiritual life — supported Norman rule

questions.question: What was the significance of the Battle of Hastings?
options: A) It established the feudal system in Europe
B) William of Normandy defeated Harold II and became King of England — transforming English society
C) It ended Viking rule in England
D) It established Magna Carta
questions.explanation: Hastings (1066) was a turning point — the Norman Conquest brought a new ruling class, language (French/Latin), and cultural influences.

questions.question: What was the feudal system?
options: A) A democratic voting system
B) A system of land ownership based on loyalty and military service — King→barons→knights→peasants
C) A church-based legal system
D) A trading system between England and France
questions.explanation: The feudal system was a hierarchy of land tenure — land was granted in exchange for loyalty and military service.

questions.question: What was the Domesday Book?
options: A) A record of William's battles
B) A Norman legal code
C) William I's survey of England — recording land, resources, and value for taxation
D) A record of the feudal system's rules
questions.explanation: The Domesday Book (1086) was William's great survey — a record of who owned what land and how much it was worth, primarily for tax assessment.

questions.question: What was the purpose of Norman castles?
options: A) To house the local population
B) To serve as centres of worship
C) To control the population and demonstrate Norman military power
D) To store crops and taxes
questions.explanation: Norman castles were symbols of power and military control — they allowed small numbers of Normans to dominate a much larger English population.

questions.question: What was Magna Carta?
options: A) William I's conquest agreement
B) A legal code imposed by William
C) The Great Charter (1215) limiting the king's power — signed by King John
D) A treaty with France
questions.explanation: Magna Carta (1215) was forced on King John by his barons — it limited royal power and established that even the king was subject to the law.

questions.question: What did the feudal system require of knights?
options: A) Only financial payments
B) Labour services for the lord
C) Military service in exchange for land
D) Service in the Church
questions.explanation: Knights held land from barons in exchange for military service — they were obligated to fight for their lord when called.

questions.question: How did William control Anglo-Saxon England?
options: A) By adopting Anglo-Saxon culture entirely
B) By building castles, redistributing land to Normans, and imposing a new ruling class
C) By allowing Anglo-Saxons to keep their land
D) By marrying into Anglo-Saxon nobility
questions.explanation: William used a combination of military force, castle-building, and wholesale redistribution of land to Norman barons to control England.

questions.question: What were the two types of early Norman castle?
options: A) Stone keeps and fortresses
B) Motte and bailey, then stone castles
C) Tower houses and ring forts
D) Hill forts and palisades
questions.explanation: Initially Normans built quick motte-and-bailey castles (earth mound + wooden keep). Later, permanent stone castles replaced them.

questions.question: Why was the Church important in Norman England?
options: A) It funded military campaigns
B) It controlled education, legal courts, and spiritual life — supporting Norman authority
C) It had no political role
D) It was directly controlled by the barons
questions.explanation: The Church was a powerful partner in Norman rule — it legitimised William's conquest (the Pope had blessed it) and controlled education and moral life.

questions.question: What happened to Harold II at Hastings?
options: A) He fled to France
B) He was captured and imprisoned
C) He was killed — possibly by an arrow
D) He surrendered and was exiled
questions.explanation: Harold II was killed during the Battle of Hastings — tradition says by an arrow to the eye, though this is debated. His death ended Anglo-Saxon rule.

questions.question: The Battle of Hastings took place in 1066.
questions.explanation: William of Normandy defeated Harold II at Hastings on 14 October 1066.

questions.question: The feudal system placed the Church at the top.
questions.explanation: The feudal hierarchy was: King → barons/lords → knights → peasants. The Church existed alongside but not above the king.

questions.question: The Domesday Book was used for taxation purposes.
questions.explanation: William commissioned the Domesday Book (1086) to assess land ownership and value for tax collection.

questions.question: Magna Carta was signed voluntarily by King John.
questions.explanation: King John was FORCED to sign Magna Carta by rebellious barons in 1215 — he signed it reluctantly.

questions.question: Norman castles were symbols of military power and control.
questions.explanation: Castles allowed the Normans to dominate a much larger Anglo-Saxon population — they were centres of power.

questions.question: Under the feudal system, peasants owned their land.
questions.explanation: Peasants (serfs) were bound to the land and owned nothing — they owed labour services to their lord in exchange for their plots.

questions.question: The Battle of Hastings ended Viking rule in England.
questions.explanation: Hastings ended Anglo-Saxon rule — it was the Norman Conquest. Viking influence had been declining since Canute's death in 1035.

questions.question: William the Conqueror redistributed English land to Norman barons.
questions.explanation: William gave Anglo-Saxon land to his Norman followers — creating a new Norman ruling class and breaking Anglo-Saxon resistance.

questions.question: Motte and bailey castles were made of stone.
questions.explanation: Motte and bailey castles were initially built from earth and wood — stone castles came later.

questions.question: Magna Carta established that the king was above the law.
questions.explanation: Magna Carta established the OPPOSITE — that even the king must follow the law.

questions.question: The Battle of ________ (1066) ended Anglo-Saxon rule of England.
questions.explanation: William of Normandy defeated Harold II — transforming England's language, culture, and ruling class.

questions.question: The ________ system organised society from King to barons to knights to peasants.
questions.explanation: The feudal system was based on land ownership, loyalty, and military service.

questions.question: The ________ Book (1086) was William's survey of England — recording land and value for taxation.
questions.explanation: The Domesday Book was a comprehensive record of English land ownership.

questions.question: ________ Carta (1215) limited the king's power — signed by King John under pressure from barons.
questions.explanation: Magna Carta was a landmark in the development of constitutional government.

questions.question: Norman ________ were centres of military power and symbols of conquest.
questions.explanation: Castles allowed small numbers of Normans to control a much larger Anglo-Saxon population.

questions.question: ________ II was the last Anglo-Saxon king — killed at Hastings.
questions.explanation: Harold's death at Hastings ended the Anglo-Saxon dynasty.

questions.question: ________ the Conqueror became William I of England after Hastings.
questions.explanation: William immediately began imposing Norman rule — building castles and redistributing land.

questions.question: Peasants (______) were bound to the land and owed labour to their lord.
questions.explanation: Serfs were at the bottom of the feudal hierarchy — they had few rights.

questions.question: Motte and ________ castles were the first Norman fortifications — quick to build from earth and wood.
questions.explanation: Once control was established, stone castles replaced the temporary motte and bailey structures.

questions.question: The ________ legitimised William's conquest and controlled education and spiritual life.
questions.explanation: The Pope had blessed William's invasion — the Church was a powerful ally in Norman rule.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: William defeats Harold II — Norman Conquest begins
King → barons → knights → peasants — land for loyalty
survey of England for taxation
King John forced to limit royal power
centres of military power and control
